key_insight: The paper shows that the long-standing mismatch between measured and predicted pressure drops in dilute polymer channel flows is largely explained by measurement interpretation and experimental-condition mismatches, not a fundamental failure of constitutive theory.
engineering_relevance: This clarifies how to correctly benchmark viscoelastic flow models and pressure measurements in microfluidic and channel-flow systems used in polymer processing, lab-on-chip devices, and drag-related applications.
method: Compared two pressure-sensing approaches: recessed pressure taps and flush-mounted diaphragm sensors.
Tested multiple channel geometries and analyzed results using the largest relaxation time.
evidence_signal: Across different channel geometries, the new measurements qualitatively agree with theory when analyzed with the largest relaxation time, unlike earlier reports that showed larger-than-predicted pressure drops.
watch_item: Quantitative agreement still depends on careful handling of hole-pressure effects, relaxation-time selection, and possible flow instabilities, so sensor and model assumptions must be matched closely.
paper_title: Understanding the Theory--Experiment Discrepancy in Pressure Drop of Dilute Polymer Solutions in Channel Flows
